content.text: Azure security follows a defense-in-depth model: multiple independent layers of security controls, so that a failure or bypass at one layer does not result in a complete compromise. Microsoft manages physical datacenter security; you configure the identity, network, compute, application, and data security layers using Azure's security services. Each layer has dedicated Azure services that provide detection, prevention, and response capabilities.

attrs.alt: Azure security defense-in-depth layers: Physical (Microsoft-managed), Identity (Entra ID), Network (Firewall/NSG/DDoS/WAF), Compute (Defender for Servers/JIT), Application & Data (Key Vault/TDE), Monitoring (Sentinel/Defender for Cloud)

content.text: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the identity and access management platform for Azure. Every Azure subscription is backed by an Entra ID tenant. Users, service principals, managed identities, and groups are objects in Entra ID. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) assignments in Azure use Entra ID identities as the principal.

requires a second verification factor (authenticator app, phone call, FIDO2 key) when a user signs in. Enforced via Conditional Access policies that evaluate sign-in context (user, device, location, application).

policy engine that evaluates real-time signals (user risk level, sign-in risk, device compliance, network location) and enforces controls like MFA, device compliance, or session restrictions. Requires Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2.

just-in-time (JIT) elevation for privileged roles. Users request role activation for a limited time period; approvers can be required; all activations are logged. Prevents standing privilege — nobody holds Owner or Global Admin permanently.

system-assigned or user-assigned identities that Azure services (VMs, Functions, AKS pods) can use to authenticate to other Azure services without credentials in code or configuration. The identity's lifecycle is managed by Azure; you assign RBAC roles to it.

# Assign the Reader role to a managed identity on a resource group
IDENTITY_OID=$(az identity show -g myRG -n myAppIdentity --query principalId -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG"

# List all role assignments for a specific service principal
az role assignment list \
  --assignee "$IDENTITY_OID" \
  --all \
  --query "[].{role:roleDefinitionName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

# Enable PIM-managed Just-in-Time activation for a user (requires Entra ID P2)
# PIM is managed via the Azure Portal or Microsoft Graph API; az cli doesn't expose all PIM operations.

# Create a system-assigned managed identity on a VM
az vm identity assign \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM

# Get the object ID of the VM's system-assigned identity
az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --query "identity.principalId" \
  --output tsv

# Grant the VM's managed identity access to a Key Vault secret
az keyvault set-policy \
  --name mykeyvault \
  --object-id $(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM --query "identity.principalId" -o tsv) \
  --secret-permissions get list

Azure Key Vault is a centralized secrets management service for storing and controlling access to secrets (API keys, passwords, connection strings), cryptographic keys (RSA, EC, AES), and certificates (X.509). By moving secrets from application configuration files and environment variables into Key Vault, you eliminate secrets from source code, reduce the blast radius of a compromise, and gain an audit trail of every access.

Key Vault has two tiers: Standard (software-protected keys) and Premium (HSM-backed keys using FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated HSMs). Azure Managed HSM provides dedicated single-tenant HSM clusters for the highest security requirements (FIPS 140-2 Level 3).

# Create a Key Vault
az keyvault create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mykeyvault \
  --location eastus \
  --sku standard \
  --enable-rbac-authorization true \
  --enable-soft-delete true \
  --soft-delete-retention-days 90

# Store a secret
az keyvault secret set \
  --vault-name mykeyvault \
  --name "DatabaseConnectionString" \
  --value "Server=mysqlserver.database.windows.net;Database=mydb;..."

# Retrieve a secret (requires Key Vault Secrets User role)
az keyvault secret show \
  --vault-name mykeyvault \
  --name "DatabaseConnectionString" \
  --query value \
  --output tsv

# Grant a managed identity the Key Vault Secrets User role
az role assignment create \
  --role "Key Vault Secrets User" \
  --assignee $(az webapp identity show -g myRG -n mywebapp --query principalId -o tsv) \
  --scope $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n mykeyvault --query id -o tsv)

# Create an RSA key (for encryption operations)
az keyvault key create \
  --vault-name mykeyvault \
  --name myEncryptionKey \
  --kty RSA \
  --size 4096 \
  --ops encrypt decrypt wrapKey unwrapKey

# Import a certificate (PFX format) into Key Vault
az keyvault certificate import \
  --vault-name mykeyvault \
  --name myWebCert \
  --file /path/to/cert.pfx \
  --password "pfxpassword"

# Enable Key Vault private endpoint (restricts access to VNet only)
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name kvPrivateEndpoint \
  --vnet-name myVNet \
  --subnet DataSubnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id $(az keyvault show -g myRG -n mykeyvault --query id -o tsv) \
  --group-id vault \
  --connection-name kv-connection

Microsoft Defender for Cloud (formerly Azure Security Center + Azure Defender) is the unified security posture management (CSPM) and cloud workload protection platform (CWPP) for Azure. It provides a security score — a percentage representing how well your Azure environment follows security best practices — and a set of security recommendations grouped by severity. Enabling Defender plans activates threat detection for specific resource types.
Azure VMs and on-prem servers via Azure Arc
Just-in-time VM access, endpoint detection, vulnerability assessment, file integrity monitoring
Malware scanning, sensitive data discovery, anomalous access alerts
Azure SQL Database, SQL MI, SQL on VMs
SQL injection alerts, anomalous access, vulnerability assessment
Kubernetes runtime threat detection, image vulnerability scanning, misconfiguration detection
Malicious scripts detection, command injection, suspicious domain communications
Unusual access patterns, access from anonymizing IPs, key exfiltration attempts

# Enable Defender for Servers (Standard tier) across the subscription
az security pricing create \
  --name VirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard

# Enable Defender for Storage
az security pricing create \
  --name StorageAccounts \
  --tier Standard

# Enable Defender for SQL (covers SQL Database and SQL Managed Instance)
az security pricing create \
  --name SqlServers \
  --tier Standard

az security pricing create \
  --name SqlServerVirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard

# View the current Defender plans and their tiers
az security pricing list \
  --query "[].{name:name, tier:pricingTier}" \
  --output table

# Get the current security score
az security secure-score show \
  --name ascScore \
  --query "{score:properties.score.current, max:properties.score.max, pct:properties.score.percentage}" \
  --output json

# List high-severity security recommendations
az security assessment list \
  --query "[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy' && properties.metadata.severity=='High'].{name:properties.displayName, resource:properties.resourceDetails.id}" \
  --output table

# Enable Just-in-Time VM access (blocks management ports, allows time-limited exceptions)
az security jit-policy create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myJITPolicy \
  --location eastus \
  --virtual-machines '[
    {
      "id": "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM",
      "ports": [
        {"number": 22, "protocol": "TCP", "allowedSourceAddressPrefix": "*", "maxRequestAccessDuration": "PT3H"},
        {"number": 3389, "protocol": "TCP", "allowedSourceAddressPrefix": "*", "maxRequestAccessDuration": "PT3H"}
      ]
    }
  ]'

Microsoft Sentinel is a cloud-native Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and Security Orchestration, Automation, and Response (SOAR) platform. It ingests logs from across Azure, Microsoft 365, on-premises infrastructure, and third-party security tools; runs analytics rules to detect threats; and provides playbooks (Azure Logic Apps) to automate responses like blocking an IP, disabling a compromised account, or creating a ticket in ServiceNow.

Sentinel is priced per GB of data ingested, with a commitment tier discount for high-volume organizations. It stores data in a Log Analytics Workspace. The first 90 days of data from Microsoft 365 Defender, Entra ID, and Azure Activity are free to ingest into Sentinel.

# Create a Log Analytics Workspace for Sentinel
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --location eastus \
  --sku PerGB2018 \
  --retention-time 90

# Enable Sentinel on the workspace
az sentinel onboarding-state create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --name default

# Connect the Azure Activity log data connector (audit events from across subscription)
az sentinel data-connector create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --data-connector-id AzureActivity \
  --etag "*"

# List all active data connectors
az sentinel data-connector list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --query "[].{name:name, kind:kind, state:properties.dataTypes.alerts.state}" \
  --output table

# List active analytics rules (threat detection rules)
az sentinel alert-rule list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --query "[?properties.enabled==true].{name:properties.displayName, severity:properties.severity, tactics:properties.tactics[0]}" \
  --output table

# List recent incidents
az sentinel incident list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --workspace-name mySentinelLAW \
  --query "[?properties.status!='Closed'].{title:properties.title, severity:properties.severity, status:properties.status, created:properties.createdTimeUtc}" \
  --output table
Azure provides two tiers of DDoS protection:

automatic platform-level protection that defends all Azure public IP addresses against volumetric DDoS attacks. This is enabled by default for every Azure subscription at no charge. It uses Azure's global network capacity to absorb attack traffic before it reaches your resources. No configuration required.

enhanced protection for specific VNets, with adaptive tuning to the normal traffic pattern of your resources, attack telemetry and diagnostics, real-time metrics during attacks, post-attack reports, and access to the DDoS Rapid Response team (a Microsoft security team that assists during active attacks). Priced per protected VNet per month.

# Enable DDoS Network Protection Standard on a VNet
# First create a DDoS Protection Plan
az network ddos-protection create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDDoSPlan \
  --location eastus

# Associate the plan with the VNet
az network vnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVNet \
  --ddos-protection true \
  --ddos-protection-plan myDDoSPlan

# View DDoS telemetry metrics on a Public IP during/after an attack
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource $(az network public-ip show -g myRG -n myPublicIP --query id -o tsv) \
  --metric "IfUnderDDoSAttack" "DDoSTriggerTCPPackets" "DDoSTriggerUDPPackets" \
  --interval PT5M \
  --start-time "$(date -u -d '-1 hour' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --end-time "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)" \
  --output table

Azure Policy is the governance service that enforces organizational standards and assesses compliance at scale. A policy definition is a JSON rule that evaluates whether resource configurations match a required state. Policy assignments apply definitions (or initiatives — groups of definitions) to a scope (management group, subscription, or resource group). Policy effects control what happens when a rule is violated:
blocks creation or modification of resources that violate the policy rule. The API call returns an error.
allows the operation but logs a compliance event. Does not block anything — used for visibility before enforcing a Deny.
automatically deploys a related resource if it doesn't exist (e.g., deploy a diagnostic setting on every storage account created).
adds or changes tags or properties on resources as part of create/update operations.
audits if a related resource is absent (e.g., report VMs that don't have the Azure Monitor Agent extension).

# Assign the built-in "Require a tag on resources" policy to a resource group
az policy assignment create \
  --name RequireCostCenterTag \
  --policy "/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG" \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "CostCenter"}}'

# Assign an initiative (set of policies) for regulatory compliance (e.g., CIS Azure Benchmark)
az policy set-definition show \
  --name "1a5bb27d-173f-493e-9568-eb56638dde4d" \
  --query displayName

az policy assignment create \
  --name CISBenchmark \
  --policy-set-definition "1a5bb27d-173f-493e-9568-eb56638dde4d" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/SUB_ID"

# Check compliance state for a policy assignment
az policy state list \
  --policy-assignment RequireCostCenterTag \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[].{resource:resourceId, compliant:complianceState}" \
  --output table

# Trigger an on-demand compliance evaluation scan
az policy state trigger-scan \
  --resource-group myRG

# Create a custom policy definition (deny creation of public IP addresses)
az policy definition create \
  --name DenyPublicIPs \
  --display-name "Deny creation of Public IP addresses" \
  --description "Prevents users from creating public IP addresses in this subscription" \
  --mode All \
  --rules '{
    "if": {
      "field": "type",
      "equals": "Microsoft.Network/publicIPAddresses"
    },
    "then": {
      "effect": "deny"
    }
  }' \
  --params '{}'

Azure Firewall is a managed, cloud-native, stateful network security service that protects Azure VNet resources. Unlike NSGs (which operate at the NIC and subnet level with simple allow/deny rules), Azure Firewall is a full Layer 3 to Layer 7 firewall with threat intelligence feed integration, IDPS (Intrusion Detection and Prevention System), DNS proxy, URL filtering, and TLS inspection. It is deployed into a dedicated subnet (AzureFirewallSubnet) and operates in hub-spoke or Virtual WAN topologies where all traffic from spoke VNets is routed through the firewall.
Azure Firewall has two SKUs: Standard (L4 FQDN filtering, threat intelligence, DNS proxy) and Premium (adds IDPS, URL filtering, TLS inspection for deep packet inspection of encrypted traffic).

# Create the firewall subnet in the hub VNet
az network vnet subnet create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name hubVNet \
  --name AzureFirewallSubnet \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/26

# Create a public IP for the firewall
az network public-ip create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name firewallIP \
  --sku Standard \
  --allocation-method Static

# Create Azure Firewall (Standard SKU)
az network firewall create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFirewall \
  --location eastus \
  --sku-tier Standard \
  --threat-intel-mode Alert

# Assign a public IP to the firewall
az network firewall ip-config create \
  --firewall-name myFirewall \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name firewallConfig \
  --public-ip-address firewallIP \
  --vnet-name hubVNet

# Get the firewall's private IP (used as the route target for spoke VNets)
az network firewall show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myFirewall \
  --query "ipConfigurations[0].privateIPAddress" \
  --output tsv

# Create a network rule collection: allow spoke VMs to reach DNS and NTP
az network firewall network-rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --firewall-name myFirewall \
  --collection-name AllowSpokeToInternet \
  --priority 200 \
  --action Allow \
  --name AllowDNS \
  --protocols UDP \
  --source-addresses "10.1.0.0/24" \
  --destination-addresses "*" \
  --destination-ports 53

# Create an application rule: allow web VMs to access only specific FQDNs
az network firewall application-rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --firewall-name myFirewall \
  --collection-name AllowAzureMonitor \
  --priority 300 \
  --action Allow \
  --name AllowMonitorEndpoints \
  --protocols https=443 \
  --source-addresses "10.1.0.0/24" \
  --target-fqdns "*.monitoring.azure.com" "*.ods.opinsights.azure.com" "*.oms.opinsights.azure.com"

# Route spoke VNet traffic through the firewall (User Defined Route)
FIREWALL_IP=$(az network firewall show -g myRG -n myFirewall --query "ipConfigurations[0].privateIPAddress" -o tsv)

az network route-table create --resource-group myRG --name spokeRouteTable --location eastus

az network route-table route create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --route-table-name spokeRouteTable \
  --name ToFirewall \
  --address-prefix 0.0.0.0/0 \
  --next-hop-type VirtualAppliance \
  --next-hop-ip-address "$FIREWALL_IP"

az network vnet subnet update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vnet-name spokeVNet \
  --name WorkloadSubnet \
  --route-table spokeRouteTable

The Web Application Firewall is a Layer 7 security feature available on Azure Application Gateway and Azure Front Door. It protects web applications from common web exploits (OWASP Top 10: SQL injection, cross-site scripting, command injection, etc.) using managed rule sets that Microsoft keeps updated. WAF operates in Detection mode (logs, does not block) or Prevention mode (logs and blocks matched requests).

# Enable WAF on an Application Gateway (WAF_v2 SKU required)
# Create WAF policy with OWASP 3.2 rule set
az network application-gateway waf-policy create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWAFPolicy \
  --location eastus

az network application-gateway waf-policy managed-rule rule-set add \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --type OWASP \
  --version 3.2

# Switch WAF from Detection to Prevention mode
az network application-gateway waf-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWAFPolicy \
  --state Enabled \
  --mode Prevention

# Add a custom rule: block requests with a specific User-Agent header
az network application-gateway waf-policy custom-rule create \
  --policy-name myWAFPolicy \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name BlockBadBot \
  --priority 10 \
  --rule-type MatchRule \
  --action Block \
  --match-conditions '[
    {
      "matchVariables": [{"variableName": "RequestHeaders", "selector": "User-Agent"}],
      "operator": "Contains",
      "negationCondition": false,
      "matchValues": ["badbot/1.0"]
    }
  ]'

# View WAF logs (in Log Analytics after diagnostic settings enabled)
# az monitor log-analytics query \
#   --workspace <LAW_ID> \
#   --analytics-query "AzureDiagnostics | where Category=='ApplicationGatewayFirewallLog' | where action_s=='Blocked' | summarize count() by ruleId_s | order by count_ desc | limit 20" \
#   --output table

The shared responsibility model defines what security tasks Azure manages vs what customers are responsible for. Understanding this boundary is essential for building a complete security posture — assuming Azure handles more than it does is the root cause of many cloud security gaps.
* For IaaS VMs: Azure manages the hypervisor and physical host; you manage the guest OS (patching, hardening, endpoint protection).

The most common gap: customers assume that moving to PaaS means they no longer need to manage identity, data classification, or application security. These remain customer responsibilities regardless of the service model. Azure provides the tools (Entra ID, Defender for Cloud, Key Vault, WAF); customers must configure and use them.

Azure security services implement defense in depth across six layers: physical, identity, network, compute, application/data, and monitoring. Each layer has purpose-built Azure services that provide detection, prevention, and response.
Microsoft Entra ID is the identity foundation: MFA and Conditional Access protect authentication; PIM eliminates standing privilege; Managed Identities eliminate credentials in code.
Azure Key Vault centralizes secret, key, and certificate management with audited access, RBAC authorization, and HSM-backed options for the highest security requirements.
Microsoft Defender for Cloud provides continuous security posture scoring and workload threat detection; enabling Defender plans per resource type activates runtime threat detection.
Microsoft Sentinel is the cloud-native SIEM+SOAR platform: it ingests logs, runs analytics rules, surfaces incidents, and automates responses via Logic App playbooks.
DDoS Network Protection Standard adds adaptive tuning, attack telemetry, and Rapid Response team access on top of the free basic protection available by default for all public IPs.
Network security (NSG, Azure Firewall, WAF, Private Endpoints) is covered in the Networking Services topic; compute security (disk encryption, JIT VM access) is configured in Defender for Servers.
